coredata.dc:description: To explore novel fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with superior sensitivity, unique function and good excellent stability for information security and substance-specific detection from low-cost and readily accessible substrates remains a challenge. In this paper, an efficient nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) for Fe3+ and glyphosate (Gly) detection employing dialdehyde starch and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene as precursors was developed through a simple hydrothermal process. N-CQDs can highly sensitive and selective recognize Fe3+ to cause fluorescence emission enhance and visual color change from blue-green to red, and its limit of detection (LOD) is 0.024 μmol L−1. Moreover, the conjugate of N-CQDs and Fe3+ may efficiently identify and detect Gly with LOD = 0.036 μmol L−1 in aqueous solution. Theoretical calculations and mechanistic studies suggest that the fluorescence switching is attributed to the combination of functional groups in N-CQDs surface with Fe3+ to generate chelation induced fluorescence enhancement effect (CHEF). In addition, the conjugate system of N-CQDs and Fe3+ can further combine with stronger coordination ability of Gly to result in fluorescence quenching. Investigations for practical applications demonstrate that N-CQDs may have significant application prospects in the fields of anti-counterfeiting, solid-state fluorescent materials, bio-imaging, and intelligent environmental detection.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots based on nitrogen doped dialdehyde starch for highly selective Fe3+/glyphosate detection and its applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, GuizhouMinzuUniversity, Guiyang 550025, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, GuizhouMinzuUniversity, Guiyang 550025, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To explore novel fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with superior sensitivity, unique function and good excellent stability for information security and substance-specific detection from low-cost and readily accessible substrates remains a challenge. In this paper, an efficient nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) for Fe3+ and glyphosate (Gly) detection employing dialdehyde starch and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene as precursors was developed through a simple hydrothermal process. N-CQDs can highly sensitive and selective recognize Fe3+ to cause fluorescence emission enhance and visual color change from blue-green to red, and its limit of detection (LOD) is 0.024 μmol L−1. Moreover, the conjugate of N-CQDs and Fe3+ may efficiently identify and detect Gly with LOD = 0.036 μmol L−1 in aqueous solution. Theoretical calculations and mechanistic studies suggest that the fluorescence switching is attributed to the combination of functional groups in N-CQDs surface with Fe3+ to generate chelation induced fluorescence enhancement effect (CHEF). In addition, the conjugate system of N-CQDs and Fe3+ can further combine with stronger coordination ability of Gly to result in fluorescence quenching. Investigations for practical applications demonstrate that N-CQDs may have significant application prospects in the fields of anti-counterfeiting, solid-state fluorescent materials, bio-imaging, and intelligent environmental detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron as a prevalent transition metal in cellular systems plays a crucial role in numerous biological processes containing oxygen transport, enzyme catalysis and cellular metabolism, etc [1,2]. Iron deficiency and excess disturbs the balance of cells to cause various biological disorders [3,4], and abnormal iron concentrations can lead to serious illnesses such as abdominal pain, Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease. Glyphosate (Gly) as a broad-spectrum, non-selective herbicide and plant growth regulator presents satisfactory performance and effect in weed and vegetation control. Gly has become the most frequently used herbicide in agricultural, forestry, and aquatic applications around whole world in view of its relatively lower toxicity to mammals [5,6]. However, the extensive application of Gly may easily bring high-level residues in agricultural products, which can bring threat to humans. Gly can cause liver, kidney, digestive tract, skin and mucous membranes damage, and it can also injure the respiratory and cardiovascular systems to endanger human life in severe cases. Fe3+ and Gly can enter organisms through accumulation effects to cause irreversible damage to biological health, and also contaminate to soil and water resources [7–10]. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore a rapid and accurate determination method for Fe3+ and Gly concentration in environment and organisms.

Sensing techniques are facing new requirements and challenges in modern science and technology, such as higher selectivity and sensitivity, rapid response, visual detections in complicated practical samples. Many analytical methods for Fe3+ and Gly had been reported in the past few decades. Among the various detection techniques, fluorescence sensors have obtained widespread attention due to the advantages such as high sensitivity, rapid time-response, and ease of operations [11–16]. At present, fluorescent probes have become an essential tool in the fields of chemical sensing, environmental analysis, foods testing and disease diagnosis. Many fluorescence sensors for Fe3+ and Gly detection, such as, small molecules, organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots, metal–organic frameworks, metal-nanoclusters have been developed. So far, most of the conventional sensors reported are based on organic molecules with multiple post-recognition groups. The conjugated structure of organic molecules presents a negative influence on the sensitivity and accuracy of the measurement to restrict their practical application [17,18]. Recently, photoluminescent carbon nanodots have received great attention in view of their high resistance to aqueous solubility, excellent biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence properties, satisfactory photo-stability, low toxicity and simple synthetic process and surface modification, and they have shown great potential in fluorescent sensing, message encryption and bio-imaging [19–24].

Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a novel carbon-based nanomaterial, has aroused arousing great interest in recent years, and significant achievements have been made in the accurate detection of Fe3+ and Gly. In 2022, Cao and coworkers developed a CQDs derived from anthracite coal for the rapid, highly-sensitive and fluorescence determinations of Fe3+ with the detection limits of 0.019 μmol L−1 [25]. Yin et al reported a fluorescent hydrogel based CQDs composite for the isolation and determination of Fe3+, and the concentration range and detection limit for Fe3+ are 0–250 μmol L−1 and 0.115 μmol L−1, respectively [26]. Wang and coworkers established a ratiometric fluorescence carbon dots to analyze and detect Fe3+, and the linear range and the detection limit was 0.015–120.0 μmol L−1 and 5.0 nmol L−1 in real samples [27]. Natural raw material wool as the precursor, Wang et al prepared a CQDs sensor for Gly with the concentration range of 0.15–15.0 mol L−1 and the detection limit of 0.07 mol L−1 [28]. Based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect, Hu group reported a fluorescent CQDs for Gly determination, and the detection limit and linear range of the concentration was 0.6 mol L−1 and 0.02–2.0 mol L−1, respectively [29]. Zhao et al successfully prepared CQDs for Gly detection using polyacrylic acid and phosphoric acid as the precursors with a detection limit of 6.2 m mol L−1 in 2–1000 mol L−1 linear range [30]. In 2023, a nitrogen-doped biomass fluorescence CQDs for visual Gly detection was developed. Fe3+ resulted in the fluorescence quenching of the solution of CQDs, then its fluorescence was recovered after addition of Gly [10]. Probes based on carbon dots usually present impressive water solubility and selectivity, but it’s difficult to get a strong comparison before and after detection due to their mechanisms of fluorescence quenching and recovery. Moreover, there are some difficulties to achieve an ideal level for the minimum detection standards at low concentrations in practical applications [17,31–34]. At present, the development of nitrogen-doped CQDs for Fe3+ and Gly still is in its infancy, which attributes to the limitations of synthetic designs and the difficulty in the construction of accurate and efficient CQDs sensing platforms [34–38]. Therefore, it’s very necessary to develop a novel strategy for the accurate monitoring and determination of Fe3+ and Gly with good stability, highly sensitivity and selectivity, and strong contrast [15,39–42].

In view of the above limitations, the work precisely designed a novel nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) for Fe3+ and Gly detection employing dialdehyde starch and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene as precursors through a simple hydrothermal process. N-CQDs may efficiently recognize Fe3+ with amazing sensitivity to cause visible change in fluorescence emission from blue to red and wavelength red-shift. Moreover, based on the fluorescence quenching, the conjugate of N-CQDs/Fe3+ can further identify and detect Gly in aqueous solution. The mechanisms on N-CQDs for detection of Fe3+and Gly were also investigated by electrochemical analysis and DFT theoretical calculation. In addition, the application prospects for N-CQDs in the fields of anti-counterfeiting, fluorescent-emitting solid-state materials, bio-imaging, and intelligent environmental detection were also investigated.

Corn starch and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene came from Shanghai Macklin Biochem. Technol. Co. Ltd, and Jakes Chem. Co. Ltd. (Zhengzhou, China) provided Gly sample. The other chemical reagents used in synthesis and analysis were satisfied without further purification.

The material's surface morphology was characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Phenom ProX, Netherlands), and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL2010,Yongke Optical Instrument Co. Ltd. Shanghai, China) was used to observe the fine structure. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Phenom ProX, Netherlands) was employed to analyze the material's elemental distribution. The analysis of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co, USA) was used to describe the chemical states in material’s surface. The fluorescence spectra were obtained through a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Baihe Instrument Technol Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China). The FT-IR spectra were collected by FTIR-650S Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Gangdong, Tianjin). UV–vis absorption data came from ATU-1810DPC spectrometer (Gangdong, Tianjin). pH levels of the solution were investigated at a PHS-3C meter (Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument Co, Ltd, China).

As depicted in the Scheme 1 , corn starch (1.0 g) was added to sodium periodic solution (0.5 mol L−1) and stirred for 5 h at 40 °C away from light to produce dialdehyde starch. In a typical hydrothermal process, dialdehyde starch and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene were mixed in an agate mortar and ground to a uniform powder. The mixture was then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. The resulting mixture was filtered using a vacuum filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm, the filtrate was dialyzed in ultrapure water using a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. The supernatant was collected to obtain a nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-CQDs) solution and stored at room temperature. The optimized results in the synthesis of N-CQDs (feed mass ratio 1:0.9, reaction temperature 180 °C, reaction time 3 h, pH = 4.0) were shown in Tables S1 and S2. In a controlled experiment, dialdehyde starch (0.50 g) and 1, 8-diaminaphthalene (0.45 g) were used to prepare the corresponding N-CQDs according to the above process.

The prepared N-CQDs solution was diluted to 10 times with deionized water, then various metal ions including Na+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Ba2+ were added to N-CQDs solution (3 mL), respectively. Fluorescence spectra were recorded at 350 nm excitation wavelength, and the slit widths were both 5 nm under excitation and emission. The anti-interference ability of N-CQDs to Fe3+ detection was further investigated. An equal amount of Fe3+ was added to the N-CQDs diluted solution, and then different metallic ions were equally added to the solution as the anti-interference control. The emission intensity of N-CQDs solution at 635 nm was measured and recorded under 350 nm excitation [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of N-CQDs/Fe3+ probes for Gly detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain fluorescent carbon dot probe N-CQDs/Fe3+, 5 mL N-CQDs prepared and 35 μL Fe3+ solution (0.01 mol L−1) were uniformly mixed to form a red solution. In test tubes, different concentrations of Gly standard solutions were added to the N-CQDs/Fe3+ probe solution. The fluorescence emission spectra of these solutions were sequentially measured at 350 nm excitation wavelength, and the standard curve for N-CQDs/Fe3+ to Gly detection was obtained. To explore the specificity of N-CQDs/Fe3+ probe to Gly detection, potential interfering pesticides (dimethoate, paclobutrazol, abamectin, malathion) were added to the N-CQDs/Fe3++Gly solution system to investigate its anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties and DFT calculations for N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the redox properties of N-CQDs, cyclic voltammetry (CV, EA161 eDAQ) was used in a standard three-electrode system consisting of a platinum sheet as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode, and an Ag/Ag+ as the reference electrode. (Bu)4NBF4 (0.10 mol L−1) in freshly and dried dimethylformamide (DMF) was used as the electrolyte solution.

The density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted using Gaussian 09 with the B3LYP functional for ground state simulations [44]. Geometry optimizing and frequency calculations for all atoms was carried out at the 6-31G(d,p) basis set. In single-point energy calculations, Fe atoms and the other atoms were respectively utilized for LanL2DZ and 6-311G(d,p) basis set [45]. The solvation effects were investigated by the Integral Equation Formalism Polarization Continuum Model (IEFPCM) when water was used as the solvent [46]. Empirical dispersion corrected approaches, such as B3LYP-D3, were established to improve computation accuracy and account for dispersion effects on long-range potentials [47]. This method allows for a thorough examination of the binding interaction energy among N-CQDs, Fe3+ and Gly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intelligent detection of Fe3+/Gly system with hydrogel and imaging in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Indian glassy fish (Parambassis ranga) as a species was adopted to estimate the organisms imaging in vivo of N-CQDs in vivo. Parambassis ranga was fed the freeze-dried solid substance of N-CQDs which was thoroughly mixed with fish food. After 15 min, the fluorescence changes in vivo were observed under fluorescence microscope at 365 nm emission wavelength. Afterwards, Parambassis ranga fish was fed the food including Fe3+ for 20 min, and the fluorescence changes of Parambassis ranga in vivo were observed. To evaluate the biological toxicity for N-CQDs, Parambassis ranga was feed the food containing N-CQDs for 15 consecutive days, and the changes in vital signs were observed [48]. As a possible source of N-CQDs contamination, the level of 1,8-diaminonaphthalene in the N-CQDs solution detected by HPLC, which was much lower than the limit standard in GB31604.27-2016.

The water gel used in the test system from polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was prepared by the method for repeated freezing and melting. The required aqueous solution in the preparation process was respectively replaced with N-CQDs and N-CQDs/Fe3+ solutions to generate a hydrogel for detection of Fe3+ and Gly [49], then a multi-purpose hydrogel detection chip is formed. According to the obtained standard curve for the fluorescence changes of Fe3+ and Gly concentration, the RGB value in the fluorescence changes can be extracted employing 365 nm UV analyzer when different concentration of gradients are dropwise added to the obtained hydrogel chip. The RGB values of Fe3+ and Gly concentration in the hydrogel chip were compiled into an APP program written in Jara language, and the concentration of target object can be analyzed by combining the RGB values in hydrogel chip and identified by shooting with the smartphone APP [50].

In order to eliminate the influence of raw material interference on the subsequent test results, two substrates of dialdehyde starch and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene were respectively treated by the same hydrothermal method, and their carbon dots have no fluorescence emission spectra around 425 nm wavelength. But the mixture of dialdehyde starch and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene can produce a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with stronger blue-green fluorescence (Fig. 1 a).

The morphology of N-CQDs can be observed through TEM, as shown in Fig. 1b. The N-CQDs exhibit high monodispersity and uniform size distribution. It can be seen from the TEM images that the diameter of N-CQDs is mainly distributed in a narrow range (1.2–3.2 nm), and the average size was 2.2 nm (Fig. 1c). Fig. 1b shows a well-defined single nanoparticle with a crystalline structure corresponding to the (102) diffraction plane of graphitic (sp2) carbon, with a lattice constant of 0.182 nm [51]. The functional groups are responsible for the differences in the spacing between the carbon layers in N-CQDs. The variations in lattice parameters in different carbon quantum dots are because of many functional groups present on the surface of N-CQDs. These findings further provide the evidence of the graphite structures present inside the carbon nuclei of N-CQDs and also support the effectiveness of the one-pot hydrothermal method for the preparation of carbon dots.

The analysis in FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1d) can elucidate the structure and composition of N-CQDs. In dialdehyde starch (DAS), a distinct peak at 1730 cm−1 is observed to indicate the elongation oscillation of the −CO bond in the aldehyde group. However, there is no −CO peak in N-CQDs instead of introducing a characteristic −CN peak at 1628 cm−1. The results indicate that the aldehyde group on the starch chain reacts with the NH2 group from 1,8-diaminonaphthalene to form the Schiff base structure. The weak peak of N-CQDs at 3459 cm−1 may be attributed to the −OH and −NH band stretching vibrations. The distinct peak at 1422 cm−1 may be the −CN stretching vibrations, and the absorption peak at 780 cm−1 represents the −CH bond in the naphthene ring. Therefore, FT-IR analysis may confirm the successful integration between 1,8-diaminonaphthalene and dialdehyde starch, and indicating that there are functional effects on the surface of N-CQDs. The –OH, –NH2, and −CN- groups with rich oxygen and nitrogen properties on the surface of N-CQDs, and these functional groups enhance its compatibility and durability in watery environments [52]. N-CQDs also showed excellent stability, and no floating or settling nanoparticles were observed even after three months of storage in the air. Moreover, there was no significant loss of photobleaching after 3 h of exposure to a 365 nm UV lamp, indicating that N-CQDs may have good potential applications.

N-CQDs assumes a strong UV absorption centered at 325 nm, as shown in Fig. 2 a, which can be attributed to n-π* transitions of −CO and −CN caused by the capture of the excited state energy of the surface state [53]. The N-CQDs has a maximum excitation wavelength which is identified to be at 350 nm. The N-CQDs presents a strong fluorescence emission peak at 425 nm when excited at 350 nm. In addition, detailed fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths are also investigated. Obviously, the emission wavelength of N-CQDs is significantly affected by the excitation wavelength, as the excitation wavelength gradually increases from 300 nm to 440 nm, the maximum emission wavelength moves from 400 nm to 520 nm (Fig. 1b). The difference may be originated from the different sizes of N-CQDs and the distribution of multiple surface emission sites. As the excitation wavelength increases, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs first enhances and then drops, accompanied by the maximum value red-shift to 425 nm. The results indicate that the optical characteristics of carbon quantum dots are greatly influenced by the surface of fluorescent groups.

XPS analyzed the chemical states in N-CQDs as shown in Fig. 2c. The N-CQDs are mostly comprised of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, with atomic percentages of 67.86 %, 7.69 %, and 24.45 %, respectively. The analysis of high-resolution spectral C1s shows three prominent peaks at 283.9, 285.2, and 287.4 eV, related to Csp2 (−CC and −CC) and Csp3 (−CN and −CN) configurations. The distinctive peak of Csp2 at 283.9 eV, related to −CC bonds, suggests that the produced N-CQDs possess a structure similar to graphite (Fig. 2d) [53]. The high-resolution spectrum of N1 exhibits two peaks at 399.73 eV and 398.84 eV, which can be ascribed to −NH and −CNC (Fig. 2e). The high-resolution O1s spectrum reveals that the characteristic peaks with binding energies of 532.10 and 530.91 eV are related to −OH and −CO groups (Fig. 2f), which suggests that the oxygen atoms within N-CQDs are in a uniform environment [54]. The results combined with the mass spectrometry data of N-CQDs (Fig. S1), provide additional evidence for the efficient synthesis of high nitrogen-doped N-CQDs using dialdehyde starch and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene as starting materials, and the functional groups such as −NH, −CN, −OH, and −CN are found on the surface of N-CQDs. In comparison to quinine sulfate, the quantum yield of N-CQDs was obtained to be 32.2 % according to the following Eq. (I):

In the equation, the subscripts “R” and “S” represent Quinine sulfate (QBS) reference and sample, respectively. F represents the combined fluorescence intensity, whereas A stands for the absorbance in the equation. φ is the fluorescence quantum yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and stability tests of N-CQDs for Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: During the synthetic process for N-CQDs, the active groups containing imine, amino and carbonyl group from dialdehyde starch and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene were introduced to the product to be helpful for Fe3+ detection. In order to verify the selectivity of N-CQDs for Fe3+ recognition relative to various ions, the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs was tested under 10 times concentration ratio of Fe3+. The excitation wavelength was 350 nm and the range was 200–800 nm. As can be observed from Fig. 3 a, when the addition of Fe3+ solution, the blue-green fluorescence emission of N-CQDs at 425 nm obviously disappears, generating a strong bright red fluorescence emission, and followed wavelength red-shift to 635 nm. The quantum yield of N-CQDs + Fe3+ was calculated to be 33.5 % and the relevant parameters are shown in Table S3. But the other metal ions have no significant effect on the original fluorescence of N-CQDs, and similar results can also be seen in ultraviolet absorption spectra (Fig. 3b). As shown in Fig. 3c, when the other metallic ions coexists with Fe3+, N-CQDs still maintains good fluorescence emission and UV absorption. The results indicate that N-CQDs demonstrate good anti-interference ability, and other coexistence metal cations have no effect on Fe3+ detection (Fig. S2). The effects of biologically important anions and amino acids were also investigated as shown in Fig. S3, and the results confirmed that they have no interference on N-CQDs to Fe3+ detection. The above experimental data also confirmed that N-CQDs to Fe3+ detection have high selectivity and strong anti-interference potential in practical samples. In order to obtain better detection results, the related conditions of N-CQDs for Fe3+ detection had been optimized. 1 mg·L−1 of concentration of N-CQDs was selected to achieve the lowest detection limit and the reaction kinetics of N-CQDs. When 200 μmol L−1 of Fe3+ was added to the N-CQDs solution, the solution rapidly produced red fluorescence within 5 s, and the intensity reached the maximum and stabilized after 40 s (Fig. S4). UV absorption also showed a similar significant change (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. 3g, the reaction followed the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9873, rate constant K = 0.0447). In addition, the reactivity of N-CQDs with Fe3+ at different pH value (1.0–10.0) as shown in Figs. S6 and S7. When the pH value was 1.0–7.5, the N-CQDs solution added by Fe3+ to displays satisfactory fluorescence bursting effect, and its UV absorption also shows significant change. The optimal conditions for the identification of Fe3+ were 1.0 mg·L−1 of N-CQDs solution, pH = 6.0, and reaction time of 40 s. Under these conditions, the ability of N-CQDs to quantitatively detection of Fe3+ was evaluated as shown in Fig. 3d and 3e, the FL intensity of N-CQDs was gradually enhanced with the increase of Fe3+ concentration (0.1–36 μmol L−1). The limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) was calculated to be 0.024 mol L−1 and 0.072 mol L−1, respectively, which were lower than Fe3+ sensor recently reported at the concentration levels (Table S4)[38,41,42]. Moreover, N-CQDs to detection of Fe3+ shows higher quantum yield and clearer color and wavelength changes, and indicates that it may be a better sensor for Fe3+ detection (Table S5)[38,41,42].

Gly is the most common herbicide and can form stable intermediates with metal ions due to its strong coordination ability with phosphoric acid and amino acid groups. Since the N-CQDs/Fe3+ surface contains of Fe3+, the N-CQDs/Fe3+system may be used to identify and detect Gly. As expected, when Gly is added to the N-CQDs/Fe3+solution, CQDs/Fe3+ can further combine with Gly to result in the fluorescence quenching of the solution (Fig. 4 a). Moreover, the reaction kinetics of N-CQDs/Fe3+ can be determined, and 0–45 μmol/L concentrations of Gly was added to the solution, the FL intensity of N-CQDs/Fe3+ solution gradually disappeared. Moreover, the red fluorescence of N-CQDs/Fe3+ completely disappeared after 10 s of reaction. The results fitted by the relationship between reaction time and fluorescence intensity showed that the reaction followed the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Fig. S8). When the concentration of Gly reaches 45 μmol L−1, the fluorescence quenching rate is 99.1 %. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs/Fe3+ with Gly concentration showed a good linear relationship with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9978 (Fig. 4b), and the LOD and LOQ were 0.036 μmol L−1 and 0.109 μmol L−1, respectively. The effect of pH on the red fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs/Fe3+ by Gly was also investigated. The fluorescence intensity of the system was observed after 0.2, 0.3, and 0.5 μmol L−1 concentration of Gly are added at pH range from 1.0–10.0 as shown in Fig. 4c. Gly toward the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system shows good fluorescence quenching effect when pH value ranges from 1.0–7.5. Other various phosphoric acid active molecules (acetochlor, glufosinate ammonium, chlorpyrifos, quinalphos) were added to the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system to investigate its selectivity and anti-jamming ability towards Gly detection. It can be observed that there are no obvious effects on the fluorescence change of N-CQDs/Fe3+ in the presence of phosphoric acid active molecules coexisting with Gly. As shown in Fig. 4d, the other phosphoric acid active molecules have no interference on N-CQDs/Fe3+ system toward Gly detection. The results in fitting of reaction rate between N-CQDs + Fe3+ and Gly indicates that the reaction conforms to a quasi first order kinetic model (Fig. S8). Compared with the traditional reported techniques (Tables S6 and S7)[10,29,30], N-CQD/Fe3+ system for detection of Gly exhibits higher quantum yield and sensitivity and anti-interference ability.

By analyzing the cyclic voltage measurement curve of N-CQDs in solution, combined with FT-IR, UV spectrum, XPS analyses and DFT theoretical calculation, the mechanismon fluorescence changes for N-CQDs can be studied after the introduction of Fe3+ and Gly. Various functional groups like −CN resulting from the reaction between dialdehyde starch and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene, –NH2, –OH on the surface of N-CQDs, are plentiful and may readily bind with metal ions to create complexes. As shown in Fig. 5 a, FT-IR analysis indicates that the addition of Fe3+ leads to a reduction in the peak area of the amino group (1422 cm−1, −CN), and appears an expansion vibration peaks of FeN bond at 700 cm−1 to indicate that Fe3+ actually chelated with imine and amino group in N-CQDs. N-CQDs shows high sensitivity for detection of Fe3+ to present large wavelength red-shift, which may be due to the appearance of Schiff base structure imine (−CN) after N-doping, the N-CQDs containing amino and imine groups to facilitate the formation of complexes with Fe3+ [55].

The fluorescence conversion mechanism of N-CQDs was further investigated through XPS ananlysis. In the XPS spectrum, there is an obvious Fe peak on the surface of N-CQDs (Fig. S9). In addition, the peak area of −CNC and −CN in the N1s spectrum of N-CQDs + Fe3+ is much smaller than that of N1s spectrum of N-CQDs, in which a new N-Fe peak appears, which is similar to the results observed in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 5b). In addition, the binding energies of 710.15 eV and 723.25 eV represent Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2, respectively, and the two satellite peaks generated by their deconflection (Fig. 5d) to demonstrate the electron-rich environment mainly provided by Fe elements in the form of Fe3+ combined with the surface functional groups of N-CQDs. The addition of Fe3+ and Gly to N-CQDs created the conditions for fluorescence red-shift and quenching.

Comparison of the UV absorption spectra of N-CQDs solution before and after the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. 5c), the disappearance of absorption peaks at 324 nm and 311 nm and the appearance of new peaks at 228 nm wavelength indicate the formation of N-CQDs/Fe3+ complexes. When Gly is introduced into the system, the absorption peak of N-CQDs/Fe3+ complex in the UV absorption spectrum decreases, and generates a new absorption peak at 287 nm. Therefore, it can be concluded that N-CQDs combines with Fe3+ through most of the amino and imine functional groups on the surface, which further integrates with Gly to form new complexes to result in the reduction in UV absorption (Fig. 5h).

Previous studies have revealed that imine and amine group with good binding affinity for Fe3+, and N-CQDs, as a spheroidal molecule of ink crystal type, has more sites to complex with Fe3+. According to the structure of molecular crystals in the photoluminescence process, there will be a red-shift effect due to the presence of similar molecular structures in the vicinity. Thus, the fluorescence red-shift of N-CQDs mediated by Fe3+ is proposed that it is a non-radiative electron transfer mechanism (Fig. 5h). To confirm the presence of electron transfer, the energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) for N-CQDs were computed using empirical formulas:

The cyclic voltammetric curve of N-CQDs in the mixture was first determined, and the oxidation potential (Eox) represents N-CQDs (Fig. 5e). The reduction potential of N-CQDs is 0.077 eV, while the ELUMO is computed as −4.4477 eV according to formula II and III.. The HOMO level was established by combining the reduction potential (Ered) with the light energy band gap (Eg), which was obtained from the absorption edge in the range of absorption. As shown in Fig. 5f, the Eg was determined as 3.689 eV electron volts. The HOMO energy level (EHOMO) was computed as −8.166 eV employing the given data and formula IV. Upon the addition of Fe3+, a complex is formed between N-CQDs and Fe3+, leading to the separation of the d-orbitals in Fe3+. The complex's crystal field stability energy (CFSE) was determined to be 2.70 eV, as mentioned earlier [56]. Due to many complexation sites for Fe3+ on N-CQDs, the excited electronic states of N-CQDs are transferred to the d-orbital of Fe3+. The electron transition in the N-CQDs radiation form (FL emission) is influenced by Fe3+ at each complexed site, resulting in a fluorescence red-shift. Following the incorporation of Gly into the N-CQDs + Fe3+ system, the resulting complex was referred to as N-CQDs/Fe3+ +Gly double complex aggregation system is formed in view of the coordination of the amino acid group and phosphate groups of Gly with Fe3+on the N-CQDs + Fe3+system [57]. According to the calculation method of B3LYP in Dmol3 mode, the ELUMO and EHOMO energy levels of Gly are −5.138 eV and −3.876 eV respectively. The LUMO energy level of Gly is greater than that of N-CQDs, preventing the passage of the excited electron of N-CQDs + Fe3+ to the LUMO orbital of Gly, indicating that photoinduced electron transfer between the N-CQDs/Fe3+ +Gly double complex system is not feasible [58,59]. To determine why only glyphosate can quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs + Fe3+ combination, three common herbicides glyphosate, acetochlor and glufosinate ammonium were compared. The activation energies for the coordination of the three herbicides with Fe3+ were computed using density functional theory (DFT), and the results were agree with experimental data (Table S8). The coordination activation energy of Gly with Fe3+ is low, indicating that Gly has strong coordination ability. Moreover, a white turbidity became visible in the solution under natural light 10 min after Gly was added. Red fluorescence disappearance of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ complex caused by Gly dues to the aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CQDs on advanced message nncryption
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic materials that can be repeatedly encrypted and decrypted have always attracted great interest. The N-CQDs synthesized in this paper can realize multiple encryption of information through its fluorescence changes. As the first step of encryption, the encrypted information is printed on the paper chip with fluorescent ink based on N-CQDs, and the information cannot be observed under sunlight (Fig. 6 a). But the content is first decrypted by irradiation with a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp as shown in Fig. 6b. In the second decryption step, a small amount of Fe3+ solution is sprayed on the printed paper, and the encoded information can be observed to to appear significantly redden under 365 nm ultraviolet light (Fig. 6c). For the next encryption, the paper is sprayed several times with a small amount of Gly solution and then dried. Under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, all the fluorescent information disappeared and was not visible to the naked eye (Fig. 6d). The printed fluorescent identification can also serve as a conventional anti-counterfeiting tool, leveraging the reversible fluorescence changes [60]. Therefore, this low-cost and reliable fluorescence switching strategy is confirmed to have potential applications in multi-level information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application as a solid phase hydrogel sensor for recognition of Fe3+ and Gly
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the applicability and detection efficiency of N-CQDs for Fe3+ and Gly, a solid-phase PVA hydrogel detection system was developed. The tiny size and exceptional optical stability of N-CQDs allow for their uniform distribution within the hydrogel network under various conditions (Fig. S10). Moreover, the hydrogel's elastic three-dimensional lattice structure offers ample the space for N-CQDs doping. As shown in Fig. 7 , solid state sensor chips fabricated with N-CQDs doped hydrogel exhibits bright blue-green fluorescence (Fig. 7a). After the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence can clearly change from blue-green to bright red within 2 min (Fig. 7b). When integrated with Fe3+, the sensor chip exhibits uniform red fluorescence throughout (Fig. 7c), but the Gly aqueous solution can cause the disappearance of red fluorescence of the N-CQDs + Fe3+ hydrogel sensor system (Fig. 7d). Consequently, N-CQDs doped hydrogels facilitate simultaneous monitoring and detection of both Fe3+ and Gly. As shown in Fig. 7e, N-CQDs and N-CQDs/Fe3+ PVA hydrogels were fabricated into 2 mm thick sheets and integrated into portable sensor devices for detecting Fe3+ and Gly concentrations, respectively. The detection device was placed in a 365 nm UV camera box, and the fluorescence and color changes of hydrogel sensor chip combined with detection solution were captured through a mobile phone connected with a self-developed smartphone detection platform (Fig. 7f). After the range of sensor chip is determined by the fixation point on detection device, and the average RGB value within the range is identified, the concentration of detected object can be calculated (Fig. 7g and S11) [61]. It can be clearly observed that yellow Fe3+ solution was gradually removed weaken to change red employing N-CQDs doped hydrogel for detection of Fe3+. The N-CQDs-doped hydrogel solid phase sensor device offers a novel method for detecting Fe3+ and Gly, enhancing their effectiveness and ease of use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CQDs for bioimaging in organisms
ce:section.ce:para: It is essential to monitor the levels of Fe3+ and Gly in organisms for the effective use of N-CQDs. Initially, the biocompatibility and toxicity of N-CQDs were assessed using standard bioobservation methods. Indian glassy fish (Parambassis ranga) were were fed N-CQDs to assess their toxicity by observations of morphology, mobility, alterations in physiological metabolism, and mortality rates. Fish food doped with 25 % N-CQDs was selected as the daily food for sample fish. Under the condition of sample size of 30, 100 % of Parambassis ranga were still alive and had normal vital characteristics after the feeding cycle of 15 days. It can conclude that N-CQDs shows very low physiological toxicity. In the next experiment, the capacity of N-CQDs to detection of Fe3+ in Parambassis ranga was investigated using fluorescence microscopy. Fish food doped with 25 % N-CQDs was prepared in the test (Fig. 8 a). As shown in Fig. 8b and 8c, there is no fluorescence to be observed in Parambassis ranga under sunlight and UV light (365 nm) field. After the first feeding of fish feed doped with N-CQDs for 15 min, it could be seen from the fluorescence microscope magnification that bright blue-green fluorescence was mainly located in the organ (Fig. 8d). After the second feeding of Fe3+doped fish food for 20 min, there was almost no bright blue-green fluorescence signal in the main internal organ area of the fish to replaced by bright red fluorescence signal (Fig. 8e). Therefore, Fe3+ results in the color change in fish organisms indicates that N-CQDs can achieve trace detection of Fe3+ at the biological level [62].

In this work, a novel nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) employing dialdehyde starch from corn and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene as the precursors was developed via a simple, convenient, and low-cost hydrothermal process. N-CQDs with blue-green fluorescence emission shows good solubility and stability in water and extreme environments. N-CQDs can high sensitively recognize Fe3+ to cause visual color change from blue-green to red and large wavelength red-shift, and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.024 μmol L−1. As far as we know, there are fewer examples of fluorescence opening for identification of Fe3+. The conjugate of N-CQDs and Fe3+ can efficiently identify and detect Gly to result in fluorescence quenching with LOD = 0.036 μmol L−1 in aqueous solution. Theoretical calculations and preliminary mechanistic studies suggest that the combination of functional groups such as −CN-, –NH2 in the surface of N-CQDs with Fe3+ causes red fluorescence switching, and theirs conjugate can further integrate with stronger coordination ability of Gly to lead to fluorescence quenching. Solid fluorescent hydrogel based on N-CQDs through connection with a smartphone APP system can accurately and quickly discern and detect trace Fe3+ and Gly in the environment. Moreover, N-CQDs can successfully be applied to imaging of Parambassis ranga in vivo. These results indicate that there is good application prospects for N-CQDs in the fields of information encryption, trace Fe3+ and Gly detection, anti-counterfeiting, intelligent detection, living organism bio-imaging.
